content.problemStatement:
There are NNN players, numbered from 111 to NNN.

Player iii's score is defined to be gcd⁡(i,N)\gcd(i, N)gcd(i,N).

Here, gcd⁡(i,N)\gcd(i, N)gcd(i,N) refers to the largest positive integer that divides both iii and NNN.

Your task is to find an ordering of the players, from left to right, that satisfies the following conditions:


Between two players with different scores, the one with a higher score must appear somewhere to the left of the one with a lower score.
Between two players with the same score, the player with a smaller number must appear somewhere to the left of the one with a lower score.
It can be proved that these rules uniquely define an ordering of the NNN people.

Your task is to find this ordering.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
Each of the next TTT lines contains a single integer NNN — the number of players.


Output Format
For each test case, print NNN space-separated integers on a new line — the player numbers in the required preference order.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There is only one player.
Test case 222: There are two players.
Player 111 has a score of gcd⁡(1,2)=1\gcd(1, 2) = 1gcd(1,2)=1, while player 222 has a score of gcd⁡(2,2)=2\gcd(2, 2) = 2gcd(2,2)=2.
Player 222 has a higher score, and so appears to the left of player 111, making the only possible order [2,1][2, 1][2,1].
Test case 444: There are four players. It can be verified that players 111 and 333 have a score of 111, player 222 has a score of 222, and player 444 has a score of 444.
So,

Player 444 has the highest score, and so must be placed first.
Player 222 has the second highest score, and so must be placed second.
Players 111 and 333 have the same score.
Among them, 111 has the smaller number, and so must be placed ahead of 333.

Thus, the final order is [4,2,1,3][4, 2, 1, 3][4,2,1,3].
